media.alt: Straight metallic shapes with a blue gradient

hero.richText:
The vanilla foundation to build the lightweight web

Janilla leverages standard technologies and principles to let you develop overengineering-free equivalent of web applications relying on heavy frameworks.

columns.richText:
Janilla is a set of three small libraries that implement an overengineering-free stack of web technologies.
- Base: provides IoC factory, JSON parser and formatter, web client and server, MVC factory.
- Backend: provides database, data persistence, CMS API, email sending.
- Frontend: provides templating engine, CMS administration panel.

Janilla is a self-contained solution.
- The base module has no dependencies (implicitly requires only the Java base module).
- The backend and frontend modules are independent of each other and require only the Janilla base module.

Janilla does not use any external systems other than the operating system.
The database and server are also embedded.

Janilla is not necessary a framework.
All library classes can work without IoC.

Janilla is a project written in the vanilla style of standard languages.
- Server-side: Java (Java SE 25 LTS).
- Client-side: JavaScript (ES6), HTML (HTML5), CSS (CSS3).

Janilla is a compact solution.
- 3 modules.
- 12 packages.
- 90 Java classes, 33 JavaScript classes.

Janilla provides everything you need to develop overengineering-free web applications.
- IoC factory: takes care of correctly building complex objects by retrieving the appropriate dependencies (DI).
- JSON parser and formatter: builds Java objects from JSON input and vice versa.
- Web server: accepts secure connections (TLS) from browser and converts between HTTP/2 streams and request/response objects.
- MVC factory: dispatches requests to handlers that implement the business logic and return responses, typically JSON (backend) or HTML/JS/CSS (frontend).
- Database: performs read and write operations on tables and indexes saved in a SQLite file.
- Data persistence: builds the database based on the entities that define the data model and takes care of saving and retrieving objects (CRUD).
- CMS API: extends data persistence by defining documents as entities with additional features (metadata/versions/drafts) and exposes access to them via REST API.
- Email sending: provides an SMTP client.
- Templating engine: implements JavaScript object rendering without direct DOM manipulation, using standard Web Components technologies.
- CMS administration panel: allows editors to manage application content through a web interface dynamically built from the data model.

Janilla is continuously improved by rewriting increasingly complex open-source applications originally developed by others.
- TodoMVC: with the templating engine, the UI can be easily created by leveraging only the power of JavaScript and HTML rather than resorting to more cumbersome alternative techniques.
- PetClinic: data persistence allows you to ignore the database and focus on the business model and logic, while SSR is achieved by combining POJOs, Java annotations and standard HTML.
- Address Book: a simple CRUD application that can run either as two separate processes (a frontend that calls the APIs exposed by the backend) or as a single fullstack process (convenient for development).
- Acme Dashboard: features JWT authentication and implements additional CRUD features such as search and pagination.
- RealWorld Conduit: features social features such as registration, comments, favorites and followers.
- Blank Template: the application integrates with the CMS and its admin panel, with users and media modeled as document collections.
- Website Template: the CMS maintains editorial content (pages, posts, images, categories) and non-editorial content (contact forms and requests, redirect rules, search results).
- Ecommerce Template: the CMS also maintains the catalog (products and variants) and additional user data related to their profile (addresses) and purchases (carts, orders, transactions).

Janilla is free software.
- The library license is the same as the OpenJDK implementation of Java SE.
- The reimplemented applications retained the same license as the corresponding original ones.
